They also do not have to disclose that technically the consumers are not buying and owning the game but rather purchasing access rights for a limited not specified time-frame.

paragraphs.text: The current gaming market is highly unregulated. The main 2 issues are: 1) Gambling mechanics in games advertised to children (so called "loot boxes"). Fifa by EA is the prime example. 2) "Games as a service" which require an online connection to function and become entirely unusable as soon as the publisher of the game decides to shut off the servers. This happened many times regardless of the games success and profits. Often after a certain period when the publisher wants to increase profits they shut off the current game and launch a 2nd game which is nearly identical to the first. Thereby forcing consumers to start from scratch with all their progress and all purchases within the game. Any money spend for "in-game" content becomes worthless as does the money spent on the game itself. Great examples for this are "The Crew" by Ubisoft or "Overwatch" by Blizzard. I purchased both for about 60 at launch and spent money on in-game purchases. And cannot access either game anymore. As of right now the industry is not obliged to to ensure a certain lifespan for such a game. They don't have to disclose that technically the consumers are not buying and owning the game but rather purchasing access rights for a limited not specified time-frame. They also do not have to offer at least portions of the game to be accessible after they shut off their servers. Self-regulation does not work in the gaming industry. They are maximizing profits at the cost of their employees and customers (which also happen to be children and their parents).
